In contrast, cardinal addition and multiplication are commutative. Ordinals are well-ordered sets up to order-isomorphism.

questiontext.text: A function \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) is absolutely continuous if for every \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists \(\delta > 0\) such that for any finite collection of disjoint intervals \((a_i, b_i) \subset [a,b]\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\sum(b_i - a_i) < \delta \Rightarrow \sum|f(b_i)-f(a_i)| < \epsilon\)

Absolute continuity (AC) implies uniform continuity and bounded variation. The fundamental theorem of Lebesgue calculus: \(f\) is AC on \([a,b]\) iff \(f\) is differentiable almost everywhere with \(f' \in L^1\) and \(f(x) = f(a) + \int_a^x f'(t)\,dt\). AC is the right class for which the FTC holds in the Lebesgue sense.

answer.text: There exists a single interval \((a_i,b_i)\) with \(|f(b_i)-f(a_i)| < \epsilon\)

questiontext.text: A complex matrix \(U\) is unitary if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(U^* U = U U^* = I\), where \(U^* = \overline{U}^T\) is the conjugate transpose

Unitary matrices are the complex analogue of orthogonal matrices. They preserve the complex inner product (Hermitian norm) and have \(|\det U| = 1\). All eigenvalues lie on the unit circle. The unitary group \(U(n)\) is a compact Lie group. A Hermitian matrix \(A = A^*\) is unitarily diagonalizable.

answer.text: All entries of \(U\) have absolute value 1
\(U^* U = U U^* = I\), where \(U^* = \overline{U}^T\) is the conjugate transpose

questiontext.text: The series \(e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{x^n}{n!}\) converges:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) For all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) (and all \(z \in \mathbb{C}\))

By the ratio test: \(|a_{n+1}/a_n| = |x|/(n+1) \to 0\) for any fixed \(x\). So the series converges absolutely for all \(x\) (radius of convergence \(= \infty\)). The resulting function \(e^x\) satisfies \(\frac{d}{dx}e^x = e^x\) and \(e^{x+y} = e^x e^y\), which can be proved directly from the power series.

questiontext.text: A monotone function \(f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}\) can have at most:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Countably many discontinuities, all of jump type

A monotone function has one-sided limits at every point, so all discontinuities are jump discontinuities. The number of jumps greater than \(1/n\) is at most \(n(f(b)-f(a))\), so the total set of discontinuities is countable. Therefore a monotone function is Riemann integrable (its discontinuities form a countable, hence measure-zero set).

answer.text: Countably many discontinuities, all of jump type

questiontext.text: Bernoulli's inequality states that for \(x > -1\) and integer \(n \geq 1\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \((1+x)^n \geq 1 + nx\)

Bernoulli's inequality can be proved by induction. It is a useful elementary inequality. For \(x \geq 0\), equality holds only when \(n = 1\) or \(x = 0\). It implies \(\lim_{n\to\infty}(1+x/n)^n = e^x\) by a squeeze argument and is used to estimate exponential growth.

questiontext.text: A topological space \((X, \tau)\) is metrizable if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) There exists a metric \(d\) on \(X\) such that \(\tau\) is the topology induced by \(d\)

The Urysohn metrization theorem: a regular, second-countable (T3 + second countable) topological space is metrizable. Not all topological spaces are metrizable: the Sorgenfrey line (reals with lower limit topology) is not metrizable despite being regular and separable. Metrizable spaces are Hausdorff and paracompact.

answer.text: There exists a metric \(d\) on \(X\) such that \(\tau\) is the topology induced by \(d\)

questiontext.text: If \(P\) is the change-of-basis matrix from basis \(\mathcal{B}\) to basis \(\mathcal{B}'\), and \([T]_\mathcal{B}\) is the matrix of a linear operator \(T\) in basis \(\mathcal{B}\), then the matrix of \(T\) in basis \(\mathcal{B}'\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \([T]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[T]_\mathcal{B} P\)

The change-of-basis formula: \([T]_{\mathcal{B}'} = P^{-1}[T]_\mathcal{B} P\). Two matrices related this way are called similar. Similar matrices represent the same linear operator in different bases and have the same eigenvalues, trace, determinant, rank, characteristic polynomial, and minimal polynomial.

name.text: Metric Spaces - Dimension of Normed Spaces

questiontext.text: A normed vector space is locally compact if and only if it is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Finite-dimensional

Riesz's theorem: a normed space is locally compact iff it is finite-dimensional. In infinite-dimensional normed spaces, the closed unit ball is not compact (by Riesz's lemma, you can always find unit vectors with distance \(\geq 1/2\) from a given finite-dimensional subspace). This distinguishes finite and infinite dimensions fundamentally.

name.text: Sequences - Series of Functions - Term-by-Term Differentiation

questiontext.text: If \(\sum f_n\) converges pointwise to \(f\) and \(\sum f_n'\) converges uniformly, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(f\) is differentiable and \(f' = \sum f_n'\)

Term-by-term differentiation theorem: if \(\sum f_n\) converges at some point and \(\sum f_n'\) converges uniformly, then \(\sum f_n\) converges uniformly (to \(f\)), \(f\) is differentiable, and \(f' = \sum f_n'\). This is one of the key results needed for power series, which can be differentiated term by term within their disk of convergence.
